Article.ArticleTitle: Bioprospecting Artemisia roxburghiana Wall. ex Besser extracts for their potential antimicrobial, antiprotozoal, antidiabetic and anticancer potential.

Abstract.AbstractText: This manuscript revisits conventional uses of Artemisia roxburghiana Wall. ex Besser for treating hyperglycaemia, protozoal infections and cancer on scientific grounds. Secondary metabolites estimation revealed the presence of terpenoids, glycosides, tannins and saponins. Significant artemisinins were quantified in leaf n-hexane extract. Highest metal chelation, ABTS and OH scavenging were observed in leaf ethyl acetate extract (IC50=30.21 ± 1.28, 54.21 ± 1.99 and 36.97 ± 2.67 µg/mL, respectively). Stem ethyl acetate extract delayed bleaching of β-carotene (IC50=44.44 ± 1.03 µg/mL) and sequestered NO (IC50=34.37 ± 1.15 µg/mL). Maximum antimicrobial (11 ± 0.09 mm zone of inhibition against A. niger), antimalarial (IC50=18.76 ± 1.80 µg/mL), protein kinase inhibition (24 ± 1.09 mm) and potency (LD50=29.02 ± 2.08 μg/mL) were recorded for leaf n-hexane extract. Leaf methanol and distilled water extracts inhibited α-amylase and α-glucosidase (82.35 ± 3.25 and 75.25 ± 2.5%, respectively). Highest tumour inhibition (84 ± 2.50%) and antiproliferative potential against DU-145 cell line (IC50 17.41 ± 2.31 µg/mL) were manifested by leaf ethyl acetate extract. The results reported herein provide in vitro evidence of the traditional use of Artemisia roxburghiana.

AffiliationInfo.Affiliation: Department of Pharmacy, Quaid-i-Azam University, Islamabad, Pakistan.
Department of Pharmacy, Quaid-i-Azam University, Islamabad, Pakistan.
Faculty of Pharmacy, Hamdard University, Islamabad, Pakistan.
Department of Pharmacy, Quaid-i-Azam University, Islamabad, Pakistan.
Department of Pharmacy, Quaid-i-Azam University, Islamabad, Pakistan.

OtherAbstract.AbstractText: Impact statement This study represents the first comprehensive investigation of Artemisia roxburghiana and provides new evidence of its significant antimicrobial, antiprotozoal, antidiabetic, and anticancer activities. Notably, artemisinin and derivatives are identified for the first time from this species, adding important phytochemical documentation to its profile. By integrating detailed chemical analysis with broad-spectrum bioactivity assessment, this work expands current knowledge of an understudied Artemisia species and offers a strong foundation for future research, natural product exploration, and therapeutic development.Practitioner Relevance Statement The findings of this study provide valuable information for herbal practitioners, pharmacognosists, and natural-product researchers by establishing the therapeutic potential of Artemisia roxburghiana for the first time through comprehensive bioactivity evaluation. The identification of artemisinins within this species expands the evidence base for its possible medicinal applications and supports informed consideration of A. roxburghiana in herbal and complementary healthcare contexts. These insights may guide practitioners and researchers in assessing the plant’s potential utility, safety, and relevance for future formulation work, pharmacological studies, and integrative therapeutic strategies.
